title: How to build interactive experiences with canvases
summary: Canvases turn AI into interactive workspaces where you can visualize information, explore workflows, and take action across complex tasks.

title: How the 2026 World Cup affected Internet traffic
summary: We analyzed global HTTP traffic to explore how kickoff times, streaming habits, and hydration breaks reshaped online activity worldwide. From late-night traffic surges to halftime browsing spikes, here is how the world connected during the global tournament.

title: How Searchable ships customer-requested features in 30 minutes on Vercel
summary: Customer-requested features shipped in as little as 30 minutes

title: Vercel and Shopify are rebuilding Hydrogen
summary: Hydrogen made headless storefronts easy to ship, but not portable. At Vercel Ship 26 in New York, we announced that we are working with Shopify to rebuild it from the ground up, a shared bet on a more open web.

title: How we built saga rollbacks for Cloudflare Workflows
summary: Cloudflare Workflows, our durable execution engine for multi-step applications, now supports saga-style rollbacks, allowing developers to specify a compensating action for each step.do().

title: Teaching agents product design at Vercel
summary: Coding agents can produce working UI fast, but what's harder is a different shape. They can copy your product's style, match its patterns, and try to follow its conventions. What they cannot do is understand why those patterns exist. Code shows agents what shipped, not why one component, phrase, or interaction became…

title: Content Independence Day, one year on: building the business model for the agentic Internet
summary: Cloudflare’s one-year report on the "agentic internet": non-human traffic has passed half of all internet traffic, and a market for licensing content to AI is starting to form.
details: AI training crawlers jumped from 22% to 52% of crawler requests between spring 2025 and June 2026 — the crawl mix has flipped toward training.
Roughly 2.5 billion people now use generative AI regularly, a platform shift reportedly moving about twice as fast as smartphone adoption.
Over 50 publisher–AI licensing deals since 2023 show content has real economic value, though the terms are still bespoke rather than standardized.

title: Unmasking the crawls with Attribution Business Insights
summary: Cloudflare added a dashboard showing website owners how AI crawlers behave — bot-vs-human traffic, and how many times each crawler visits per referral it sends back.
details: Crawl-to-referral ratios run from 118:1 up to nearly 50,000:1, so AI crawlers take far more than they send back compared with traditional search.
Traffic is broken down by operator and crawlers are classified as training, search, or agent, with volume and bandwidth per bot.
It is meant to inform licensing negotiations and sits alongside — not inside — the existing bot-management rules.

title: GenPage: Towards End-to-End Generative Homepage Construction at Netflix
summary: Netflix built a single generative transformer that constructs a personalized homepage end-to-end, replacing its traditional multi-stage recommendation pipeline.
details: Custom tokens mapped to Netflix rows and titles, instead of a generic text tokenizer, cut sequence length ~75% and let business rules be enforced by masking tokens.
Enriching the user-context prompt cut loss ~6.9%, while scaling the model from 120M to 900M parameters gained only ~1.3% — representation mattered more than raw size.
Generating only the first few high-attention titles per row, then scoring the rest in one pass, cut end-to-end latency ~20% versus the production baseline.

title: Vercel Open Source Program: Spring 2026 cohort
summary: Vercel named the 33 open-source projects in its Spring 2026 funding cohort, each getting platform credits and support — ecosystem news rather than a technical deep-dive.
details: Each of the 33 selected projects receives $3,600 in Vercel platform credits plus support.
Selection spans frameworks, libraries, and developer tooling across the open-source ecosystem.
The "Spring 2026 cohort" framing points to a recurring, cohort-based program rather than a one-off grant.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
summary: Dropbox built an automated feedback loop with DSPy: calibrate LLM judges against human labels, then use those judges to optimize its Dash chat agent’s prompts.
details: Scoring the agent across separate dimensions — intent, tool use, context selection, grounding, task completion — shows where failures happen instead of one blunt score.
Human-labeled rubrics come first; DSPy’s GEPA and MIPROv2 then optimize toward agreement with humans, not an abstract quality metric.
Prompt edits were kept small and reviewed, doubling experiment velocity while guarding against runaway optimization in production.

title: AI SDK 7
summary: Vercel’s AI SDK 7 targets production agents: a unified reasoning option across providers, durable workflows that survive restarts, and built-in OpenTelemetry observability.
details: A single reasoning option and typed tool/runtime contexts replace provider-specific reasoning APIs and unsafe credential passing.
A new workflow package lets agents persist state across steps and survive process restarts and deploys — closing a real production gap.
Redesigned telemetry with OpenTelemetry and per-step stats gives visibility into agent behavior without wiring callbacks everywhere.

title: Introducing Meerkat: an experiment in global consensus
summary: Cloudflare is testing a new consensus algorithm called QuePaxa in a service called Meerkat, aiming to keep control-plane data consistent across data centers without a single leader node.
details: Leader-based consensus systems like Raft can stall or become unavailable when the leader fails or the network degrades, which is a bigger risk across globally distributed data centers.
QuePaxa removes the need for a single leader, letting concurrent proposals cooperate instead of triggering disruptive re-election pauses.
In tests under adversarial network conditions, Meerkat sustained roughly 10x the throughput of Raft and Multi-Paxos, showing the consensus algorithm choice matters for real-world resilience.

title: Cloudflare proudly joins the UK government's Cyber Resilience Pledge
summary: Cloudflare became a founding signatory of the UK government's Cyber Resilience Pledge, a voluntary commitment to board-level security oversight, baseline protections, and vetted suppliers.
details: The pledge asks organizations to commit to board-level accountability for cybersecurity rather than leaving it solely to technical teams.
Cloudflare ties its participation to concrete practices, such as requiring critical suppliers to hold ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II certifications.
The announcement cites UK-specific risk data, including that 43% of surveyed British businesses reported a cyber incident in the past year, as context for why pledges like this matter.

title: Your Worker can now have its own cache in front of it
summary: Cloudflare launched Workers Cache, letting a Worker cache its own responses in front of it using standard HTTP headers instead of separate CDN configuration.
details: Enabling the cache is a one-line change in the Wrangler config, and caching behavior is then controlled with familiar Cache-Control and Vary headers.
The cache is two-tiered: a nearby regional tier is checked first, then a shared upper tier, so once any location fills the cache, other regions can hit it too.
Because ctx.props can scope cache keys per user, developers can cache responses from authenticated, multi-tenant APIs without leaking one user's data to another.

title: Making AI search smarter
summary: Cloudflare is piloting ways to pay creators when AI systems use their content in search answers, instead of only rewarding pages that get crawled.
details: AI-generated search summaries sharply cut referral traffic to original sources, with one cited study finding users click through only about 8% of the time when a summary is shown.
Cloudflare says over half of the crawl traffic it sees hits pages that haven't actually changed, so its new signal-based approach aims to cut wasted crawling rather than just block it.
A 'pay-per-query' pilot with partners like Ceramic.ai would compensate publishers when their content is actually used in an answer, not just when a bot crawls it.

title: Your site, your rules: new AI traffic options for all customers
summary: Cloudflare now lets site owners separately allow or block three kinds of AI bots (search, agent, and training crawlers) instead of one all-or-nothing switch.
details: Site owners can now distinguish bots that index content for search, bots acting on a person's behalf in real time, and bots scraping content to train models.
Starting September 15, 2026, new domains will block Training and Agent bots on ad-monetized pages by default, while Search bots stay allowed, unless the owner opts out.
A new optional robots.txt signal lets sites specify how their content may be used, ranging from no storage at all to full summarization and reproduction.

title: Announcing the Monetization Gateway: charge for any resource behind Cloudflare via x402
summary: Cloudflare opened a waitlist for Monetization Gateway, letting sites charge small stablecoin payments per request for pages, APIs, or datasets using the x402 protocol.
details: The flow rides on HTTP's existing 402 Payment Required status code: a client requests a resource, gets a price back, pays, and resubmits the request with proof of payment.
Payments settle peer-to-peer in stablecoins in under a second, a design aimed at AI agents that read a resource once rather than maintain a subscription.
No account signup is required from the payer; Cloudflare's model treats the payment itself as the credential that unlocks the resource.

title: A broken DNSSEC rollover took down .al. Now 1.1.1.1 tells you when validation is bypassed
summary: When Albania’s .al domain broke from a botched DNSSEC rollover, Cloudflare’s 1.1.1.1 kept it reachable but now returns an error code so clients can see DNS security was bypassed.
details: Restoring a broken DNSSEC domain via a Negative Trust Anchor used to be silent — clients could not tell a bypassed answer from a spoofed one.
The new Extended DNS Error 33 signals in the response itself that a Negative Trust Anchor was applied, giving operators direct visibility.
Tooling is adopting it — kdig names EDE 33 and an Unbound pull request is in review — so the transparency spreads across the ecosystem.

title: Building Service Topology at Scale: Architecture, Challenges, and Lessons Learned
summary: Netflix built a real-time system that maps which services talk to which across its infrastructure, processing millions of network flow records per second instead of batching them hourly.
details: Continuous streaming with backpressure slows the pipeline to a sustainable rate under a spike or GC pause instead of collapsing — an advantage batching does not give.
A three-stage pipeline with multiple redistribution points spread load when some instances saw 100x the traffic of others; one design choice solved several problems.
They dropped immutable data structures on the hot path — a Scala norm — after profiling showed GC costing more CPU than the real work. Measure before assuming.

title: Introducing Precursor: detecting agentic behavior with continuous client-side signals
summary: Cloudflare’s Precursor detects bots by watching how a visitor behaves across a whole session, rather than judging them at a single checkpoint like a CAPTCHA.
details: Consistent human behavior over time is hard to fake, so session-long analysis catches bots that can pass an individual challenge.
Because signals accumulate across the session, refreshing the page or restarting a challenge does not reset detection.
It collects the minimum useful signal — keystroke timing, not the keys themselves — kept internal and not tied to a user’s identity.

title: Improving Smart Tiered Cache for Public Cloud Regions
summary: Cloudflare fixed a case where caching in front of anycast cloud origins sent traffic across continents, by letting customers name their cloud region so it picks the right upper-tier data center.
details: Anycast origins on AWS/GCP/Azure could hairpin traffic — an Asian request routed to Chicago to fetch from Singapore — adding hundreds of milliseconds.
A cloud region hint like aws:us-east-1, set via dashboard, API, or Terraform, tells Cloudflare which upper tier is genuinely closest.
It matches origin IPs against provider range files and tier data refreshed every 15 minutes, with geographic fallback until real latency data builds up.

title: From Day 1 to Production: Building Lyft’s Analytics & Rides Intelligence Assistant as Onboarding
summary: A new Lyft engineer took an AI analytics assistant from prototype to a company-wide service during onboarding, and writes up the production realities that onboarding decks leave out.
details: The real lessons were unglamorous production problems — auth plugin incompatibility, Envoy session config, streaming chunk sizing — that you only learn by hitting them.
Mature internal frameworks (Lyft Node Service, Envoy, metaservice) worked as real tools, letting a newcomer ship meaningfully from week one.
A staff engineer pointing to an existing service with the same streaming pattern turned a multi-day debug into an afternoon — cross-team guidance compounds.

title: Why we cannot wait for better post-quantum signature algorithms
summary: Cloudflare argues we should deploy the ML-DSA post-quantum signature scheme now despite its bulk, because better alternatives will not be widely ready until the mid-2030s — too late.
details: Better schemes (FN-DSA, SQIsign, multivariate) are not expected to be broadly available until 2033–2035, and the quantum threat arrives too soon to wait.
There is no post-quantum equivalent of an all-round winner like Ed25519 — only specialists and balanced-but-unremarkable generalists like ML-DSA.
You cannot cut over all at once, so a long period supporting both old and new signatures is needed to avoid downgrade attacks.

title: Cloudflare Internal DNS is now generally available
summary: Cloudflare now runs public and private DNS from one control plane, so internal and external name resolution stop living in separate systems that drift apart.
details: Treating internal and external resolution as two views over shared zones removes the sync drift that split-horizon DNS usually causes.
One API, control plane, and audit trail cover the whole DNS stack instead of stitching separate tools together.
Resolver policies run through Cloudflare Gateway, so which users and devices see which view is enforced by the same Zero Trust layer.

title: Content Ingestion & Podcast Video Incident Report
summary: A Spotify post-mortem: video transcoding hit capacity and delayed podcast publishing for hours, after a quality upgrade quietly raised the cost of processing each episode.
details: Plan capacity against spikes like bulk content delivery, not steady-state averages — the headroom to absorb them is the whole point.
A quality improvement raised per-episode processing cost, but capacity was not recalculated; feature changes need a downstream capacity review.
Monitoring fired about four hours before formal incident response began — early alerts need a clear escalation path, not just a dashboard.

title: How our universal content processing platform Riviera evolved for AI and beyond
summary: Dropbox rebuilt its file-processing platform around small reusable transformations instead of one pipeline per format — and that same platform now feeds its AI features.
details: Breaking each job into small reusable transformations means a PDF-to-image step written once is shared everywhere, instead of a new service per file type.
A plugin model separates request coordination from execution, so product teams add transformations without destabilising the core.
Because the platform was shared, the AI product reused existing document processing rather than rebuilding it — and one improvement benefits search, previews, and AI at once.

title: In-House LLM Serving at Netflix
summary: Netflix built its own LLM inference stack rather than only calling hosted APIs, trading raw benchmark speed for control, customization, and an easier path from research to production.
details: They chose vLLM over TensorRT-LLM because it loads custom model architectures without a compile pipeline — iteration speed can matter more than peak throughput.
Exposing an OpenAI-compatible API alongside gRPC let teams move from hosted models to self-hosted ones with almost no code change.
Production surfaced gaps the design missed: constrained decoding bottlenecked on CPU work until a newer engine version processed it batch-at-a-time.

title: Cloudflare WAF protects WordPress applications from two high-severity vulnerabilities
summary: Cloudflare added WAF rules blocking two high-severity WordPress vulnerabilities — but is explicit that the rules only buy time and are no substitute for patching.
details: A WAF rule reduces exposure while you patch, but is not a fix: upgrade affected WordPress (6.8+) to a patched release.
Check any ruleset-level overrides so the new blocking rules actually stay enabled on your site.
Watch Security Events for requests matching the rules to catch exploitation of the SQL-injection and remote-code-execution CVEs.

title: Shipyard: How We Built Slack’s Next-Generation EC2 Platform
summary: Slack replaced continuously-patched servers with immutable, versioned machine images, so instances are rebuilt from a known artifact rather than mutated in place.
details: A shared base image carries OS hardening and agents while service teams layer their own on top — global patching without duplicated foundations.
Splitting "bake" (packages into the image) from "provision" (env settings at boot) cuts startup to seconds and removes configuration drift.
An automated reaper gracefully replaces tainted or aged instances, with rate limits and a global pause button to protect capacity.

title: Vercel acquires Better Auth to accelerate open source auth
summary: Vercel acquired the open-source Better Auth library, keeping it MIT-licensed while steering it toward giving AI agents their own scoped, revocable identities.
details: Better Auth stays free and MIT-licensed under its existing name, so current users and contributors are unaffected.
The roadmap centers on agent identity — agents acting under scoped, individually revocable permissions instead of blanket trust.
Multi-framework support continues; the core team moving to Vercel is about accelerating the same open governance, not restricting it.

title: How GitHub used secret scanning to reach inbox zero
summary: GitHub drove 20,000+ exposed secrets across 15,000 repos to zero open alerts in nine months by triaging noise, checking which secrets still worked, and making hygiene a measured fundamental.
details: Five repositories held ~18,000 of the alerts (test fixtures, dead credentials) — triage the noise first and most of the work disappears.
Automated validity checks, like a read-only API call with a token, route only still-active secrets to owners rather than revoked ones.
Folding secret hygiene into an Engineering Fundamentals program teams are measured on turned a security-team silo into shared accountability.

title: Vercel Ship 2026 recap
summary: Vercel’s Ship 2026 recap centers the platform on building and running AI agents, alongside full-stack deploys (Docker, Python backends) and enterprise security features.
details: An agent stack — AI SDK, Workflow SDK, Sandbox, Connect for credentials, and the open-source eve framework — treats an agent as a directory you scaffold, run, and deploy.
Full-stack support widened: Docker containers via a registry, backends like FastAPI and Flask, and marketplace databases including Aurora and DynamoDB.
Enterprise governance arrived — Passport for identity, a Security Dashboard, and Bring Your Own Cloud for AWS.

title: How GitHub gave every repository a durable owner
summary: GitHub gave every one of its 14,000+ internal repositories a validated owner and archived 8,000 inactive ones, because unowned repos kept breaking security workflows and incident response.
details: Sync ownership from systems you already have before asking teams to fill anything in by hand — it bought day-one coverage of ~1,500 repositories.
Prefer archiving to deletion: a reversible action can be applied org-wide instead of debated repository by repository.
Give any bulk automation a low-water-mark threshold that halts it when it exceeds a conservative limit.

title: Automating cross-repo documentation with GitHub Agentic Workflows
summary: GitHub generates documentation pull requests automatically as features merge, pulling in the engineer who approved the feature as reviewer — closing the usual lag between release and docs.
details: Map milestones to release branches so the agent never has to decide where its output belongs.
Give the agent a scoped app token and route writes through a narrow handler rather than granting it direct write access.
Open drafts only, and reassign review to whoever approved the original feature — existing expertise instead of new review burden.

title: How GitHub Copilot enables zero DNS configuration for GitHub Pages
summary: A walkthrough of taking a GitHub Pages site to a custom domain with HTTPS in about 14 minutes, with registrar DNS records configured through the Copilot CLI instead of by hand.
details: Driving a registrar API from the CLI removes the manual DNS step while keeping an approval checkpoint before records change.
Build resolution and HTTP-status checks into the deploy itself so success is confirmed rather than assumed.
The skill pattern generalizes to any registrar with an API, so the workflow is not tied to one provider.

title: Introducing the new Vercel Agent
summary: Vercel’s agent now works inside the dashboard, investigating production issues and proposing fixes for approval rather than applying them directly.
details: Plan-to-permission gives the agent short-lived capability for the tasks it named, limiting the blast radius of one bad prompt.
Running generated code in a sandboxed microVM lets changes be validated against real builds before a human sees them.
Immutable deployments make autonomous action tolerable — a bad deploy is one rollback away, so the model need not be perfect.

title: How Speechify serves 500,000 dynamic pages to 60 million users on Vercel
summary: Speechify serves 500,000+ dynamic pages across 40+ languages, and describes cutting infrastructure cost roughly in half by leaning on caching rather than scaling the database with traffic.
details: Cache dynamic content — A/B variants, localized pages — so database cost stops scaling linearly with traffic.
Instant rollback changes deployment from a risk to be managed into a routine a small team can ship through daily.
Offloading patching and autoscaling to the platform is what let a small growth team keep shipping instead of maintaining.

title: Open-weight models surge to 29% of volume, price per token flattens
summary: Open-weight models now carry 29% of token volume at 4% of spend, while Anthropic takes 61% of spend on 32% of tokens — a split that argues for routing by risk rather than by default.
details: Route high-volume, low-risk work to cheap open-weight models and keep frontier models for work where being wrong is expensive.
GLM 5.2 reached the top ten within two weeks of launch, so treat model choice as swappable configuration rather than a fixed dependency.
No provider leads every modality — text, image, and video have different winners, so plan for multi-provider routing.

title: The cost of saying yes has changed
summary: AI lowers the cost of producing a first patch, but review and long-term ownership still determine whether a change is actually cheap.
details: Treat an agent-produced patch as a price check, not the finished product.
Reviewability and ownership—not initial implementation time—remain the real engineering cost.
Constrain small experiments to make scope discussions evidence-led.
